journal_entry.description: Customer deposit received for moving services to be performed in one month
journal_entry.rationale: Under IFRS 15, revenue is recognized when performance obligations are satisfied. Since the moving services will be performed in one month, the $5,000 deposit received represents a contract liability (unearned revenue/customer deposit). The cash received increases the asset (debit), and the obligation to perform services in the future creates a liability (credit). No revenue is recognized at this time because the performance obligation has not been satisfied. The transaction is not taxable at this point; HST/GST will be collected and remitted when the services are actually performed and invoiced.

output.reason: Atlas receives $5,000 cash deposit via bank transfer. This increases the chequing account (bank/cash asset). Debit: 1 asset increase. The credit side would record unearned revenue (liability increase) since the moving services have not yet been performed.

debit_classifier.reason: Atlas receives $5,000 cash deposit via bank transfer. This increases the chequing account (bank/cash asset). Debit: 1 asset increase. The credit side would record unearned revenue (liability increase) since the moving services have not yet been performed.

output.reason: Upon receiving the $5,000 deposit, Atlas has two credit-side effects: (1) Cash/chequing account increases = asset increase, but we classify CREDITS only, so this is on the debit side. (2) The deposit creates an obligation to perform services = unearned revenue/deferred revenue, which is a liability increase. Therefore, the credit side has one line: liability increase for the unearned revenue.

credit_classifier.reason: Upon receiving the $5,000 deposit, Atlas has two credit-side effects: (1) Cash/chequing account increases = asset increase, but we classify CREDITS only, so this is on the debit side. (2) The deposit creates an obligation to perform services = unearned revenue/deferred revenue, which is a liability increase. Therefore, the credit side has one line: liability increase for the unearned revenue.

output.reason: The initial credit tuple is correct. Atlas received $5,000 as a deposit for services to be performed in the future. Under IFRS, this creates an obligation (liability) to either perform the services or refund the money. This is unearned revenue, which is classified as a liability increase until the services are actually performed. The credit side correctly shows (1,0,0,0,0,0) = one liability increase line. The debit side shows (1,0,0,0,0,0) = one asset increase line (cash/chequing account), which is consistent with receiving the deposit. Revenue will only be recognized when the moving services are actually provided in one month. No correction needed.

Revenue will only be recognized when the moving services are actually provided in one month. No correction needed.

output.reason: The transaction involves Atlas receiving $5,000 cash deposit for services to be performed in the future. This creates two accounting effects: (1) Cash (asset) increases by $5,000, and (2) Unearned revenue/deferred revenue (liability) increases by $5,000, since the service has not yet been performed. The debit side correctly shows (1,0,0,0,0,0) representing the asset increase (cash received). The credit side correctly shows (1,0,0,0,0,0) representing a liability increase (unearned revenue). The two sides tell a consistent story under IFRS: cash in, obligation to perform services created. No correction needed.

output.reason: Entry correctly applies IFRS 15 revenue recognition principles. Cash debit of $5,000 properly records the deposit received. Unearned Revenue credit of $5,000 correctly recognizes the contract liability since performance obligation has not been satisfied. Entry balances (debits = credits). No revenue recognized at deposit stage is correct. Tax treatment is appropriate - HST/GST will be applicable when services are performed, not at deposit receipt. All amounts match transaction description. Complete and directionally correct.
